_note: Raw extraction — all 39 unique post-dedup entries before Phase 3 auto-removals. 5 entries marked _recommended_removal were auto-accepted and excluded from final JSON.

restaurants.name: Beluga
restaurants.quote: From Malaga rice — where Beluga gets its name — to seaweed dressed with leeks, French oysters or acorn-fed Iberian ham, the excellent cuisine takes you on a journey along the Mediterranean coastline.

restaurants.name: Casa Aranda
restaurants.quote: This is one of the most classic places in town to have churros. So many people love them that they have an entire little alleyway serving them all day long.

restaurants.name: El Pimpi
restaurants.quote: You can have some of the most traditional dishes in Malaga province. The menu changes with seasons but some favourites are always available — salmorejo, ensaladilla rusa and my personal favourite, the fritura mixta.

restaurants.name: Los Patios de Beatas
restaurants.quote: From fried calamari to Thai-style mussels or black garlic buttered anchovies, each dish encompasses the charm of Andalusia and its delicious cuisine — accompanied by over 500 wines from the cellar.

restaurants.name: Taberna Uvedoble
restaurants.quote: Enjoy a variety of delicious tapas-style dishes such as ensaladilla rusa, smoked sardines or Iberian sirloin. The chefs use local ingredients to prepare a wide selection of mouth-watering flavours.

restaurants.name: Antigua Casa de Guardia
restaurants.quote: They're known for their old barrels filled with Malaga's fortified wines. Try the pajarete (sweet, but not overly so) or a seco trasañejo for something dry — with a kick!

restaurants.name: Araboka
restaurants.quote: Owner Antonio welcomes you at the door. The ajoblanco is super famous and must-try — a cold almond-garlic soup that defines the house.

restaurants.name: Balausta
restaurants.quote: Flavoursome dishes such as garlic prawns, warm asparagus salad and pure-bred Malaga suckling goat grace the main menu, followed by a tempting array of desserts like tiramisu or roasted pineapple and coconut ice cream.

restaurants.name: Bar La Comba
restaurants.quote: Venture out to Bar La Comba for some of Malaga's yummiest fried fish, sold in paper cones. You can grab and go, or eat at their sidewalk tables — super casual but don't miss it if you love fried fish!

restaurants.name: Casa Lola Strachan
restaurants.quote: My go-to choice when I eat out in the historic centre. Our favourite tapas are their patatas bravas and Russian salad. We also always order the grilled monkfish that comes with salad.

restaurants.name: Cávala
restaurants.quote: Chef Miguel Ángel Mayor showcases local commitment through dishes like gazpachuelo and urta, revealing a Málaga that extends beyond coastal areas into valleys and mountains to uncover overlooked regional treasures.

restaurants.name: Da Saveria
restaurants.quote: Whether you adore fresh Italian pasta, pizza, chicken or fish, every dish promises a blend of exquisite flavours — eat inside or outside on the terrace opposite the Iglesia del Carmen.

restaurants.name: El Cabra
restaurants.quote: Their espetos de sardinas and roasted red peppers are to die for. I try to make a trip here every time I come to Malaga — even if it means taking a taxi straight to the train station after lunch!

restaurants.name: El Tapeo de Cervantes
restaurants.quote: Their tapas are high quality and inventive, and they have some great wines by the glass. Desserts are great too — so save room!

restaurants.name: El Tintero
restaurants.quote: The quality of food isn't quite that of the others, but the experience is completely unique. Waiters will walk around, auctioning off their plates, fresh from the grill. Most plates cost €8.50.

restaurants.name: EME de Mariano
restaurants.quote: Small and charming restaurant near the Cathedral; their cachopo should be in the Top Five of Málaga's best cachopo dishes — a must for lovers of hearty traditional cooking.

restaurants.name: FOMO Bar
restaurants.quote: I love that they use local products and make traditional dishes with a modern twist without compromising flavours. The Perrito Malagueño — brioche with salchichon and Pedro Ximenez wine — is a highlight.

restaurants.name: Kraken
restaurants.quote: I tried the pork and mushroom rice, shrimp taco, pil pil prawn croquetas and duck lasagna. The food is delicious and portions are reasonable.

restaurants.name: La Antxoeta
restaurants.quote: Tasting the refined menu by Pablo Caballero that offers textures, flavors and presentation of home cooking elevated to its highest expression, a tribute to a lifetime exploring Mediterranean cuisine.

restaurants.name: La Cosmo
restaurants.quote: Dani Carnero operates this Bib Gourmand-recognised space completing his gastronomic trio with Kaleja and La Cosmopolita. It combines classics and savoury informality with items like roasted peppers and patata rota.

restaurants.name: La Cosmopolita
restaurants.quote: Steak tartare, Malaga prawns or albondigas are just a small selection of seasonal dishes to tempt you — fresh, created with local ingredients and a touch of the exotic.

restaurants.name: La Taberna de Mike Palmer
restaurants.quote: Chef Miguel Palma operates this off-centre taberna emphasising Mediterranean ingredients. Featuring fuego lento y parrilla — even vegetables are grilled — alongside local specialties like chorizo croquetas and morcilla de hígado.

restaurants.name: Los Cuñaos
restaurants.quote: This restaurant serves the best fried fish and espetos in Malaga, and I love that the service is usually fast. The best chiringuito in Pedregalejo.

restaurants.name: Los Gatos
restaurants.quote: Visit for excellent tostas (delicious toppings on toasted bread) and vermouth on tap! Started in Madrid, Los Gatos is as Madrileño as it gets but the owners moved here and brought it with them.

restaurants.name: Los Mellizos
restaurants.quote: This fish restaurant specialises in fried anchovies with lemon, clams, grilled white fish and paella. It also offers gluten-free dishes — just talk to the waiter to make a note.

restaurants.name: Marisquería Casa Vicente
restaurants.quote: Casa Vicente is known for its shrimp — it's been selling them since the 1960s — served by the plateful with a pink bucket on the side for the shells. Sprinkled with salt and a touch of squeezed lemon, they are perfect with a cold beer any time of day.

restaurants.name: Mesón Mariano
restaurants.quote: The most famous dish here is the artichokes, which are prepared just about any way possible — grilled, fried, breaded, candied, or cooked Montillana-style with jamón ibérico. They're delicious… as is everything!

restaurants.name: Palodú
restaurants.quote: Opened in 2015 by Cristina Cánovas and Diego Aguilar, this neighbourhood restaurant maintains an essence of local community while sourcing from surrounding shops. Known for cocina joven y a dos voces — a young kitchen, two voices.

restaurants.name: Restaurante El Jardín
restaurants.quote: Operating since 1887, it maintains the flavour of times past. Ask about the daily menu — it is very well priced and has a varied offering.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Mexicano Tulum
restaurants.quote: I usually order the cochinita pibil and chicken tacos with salsa verde. They're very flavoursome — a genuine Mexican restaurant, not a chain.

restaurants.name: Rincón Catedral
restaurants.quote: Ask for Emilia and order the flagship dish of Málaga, its majesty the Gazpachuelo. Emilia's version is unmatched — there is no better in the city.

restaurants.name: Ta-Kumi
restaurants.quote: From sushi on rice to fresh sweet shrimp and wagyu beef, each dish is prepared with precision by Japanese chefs combining the flavours of the Far East with local Spanish ingredients.

restaurants.name: Taberna La Pechá
restaurants.quote: My top recommendation is to order the Ensaladilla Rusa. Personally, it's one of the best I've ever had — smooth, perfectly seasoned, a standout tapa in Málaga.

restaurants.name: Verum — el Asador de Málaga
restaurants.quote: Serving a variety of high-quality grilled meats, from cured beef to black-legged pig from Andalusia. Accompanied by succulent beef tomatoes, traditional Padron peppers and fresh Mediterranean asparagus.

restaurants.name: El Huesca
restaurants.quote: High tables on the terrace, with views of the Cathedral and the Palo Borracho tree in flower; the danger of El Huesca is that hours pass and you don't notice.

restaurants.name: La Tranca
restaurants._recommended_removal: bar-primarily (cold beer venue, no dining establishment)
restaurants.quote: Fun and filled with locals, La Tranca does cold beer and yummy empanadas. It's a place where a lot of younger people hang out, and a fun place to have a quick beer before something more substantial.

restaurants.name: Las Merchanas
restaurants.quote: Probably one of the most peculiar bars in all of Spain, this Semana Santa themed bar goes to show just how much the locals love the Easter traditions — chow down on yummy traditional tapas and drinks amid incense and virgins adorning the space.

restaurants.name: Parador de Málaga Gibralfaro
restaurants.quote: Their take on the classic crema catalana is my favourite. It's made with mangoes from the Axarquia region in Malaga — one of the best desserts I've had in the city.

restaurants.name: Pez Tomillo
restaurants._recommended_removal: quote lacks dish-name specificity (no named dish, no chef, no price)
restaurants.quote: Pez Tomillo is far from a shack but it is a great restaurant with modern interpretations of Malaga classics, and really yummy seafood too.
